tips.breathSupport: Moderate, with a gentle pulse on each 'Bah.' The B naturally creates a micro-pause in airflow — use that moment to reset your support for the next note.
tips.mouthAndJaw: Lips come together gently for the B, then open fully for 'Ah.' The jaw should drop cleanly on each repetition. Don't clamp the lips — light contact is all you need.
tips.posture: Energized but relaxed. This exercise builds vocal onset, so stand with intention — like you're about to speak clearly to a group.
tips.commonMistakes: Replacing the B with a glottal attack (silent throat closure before each note). The whole point is to use the lip closure instead of throat closure. If you hear a 'click' before each note, you're adding glottal onset.

vee-resonance.vocalInstruction: Sing 'Vee' on each note of the 5-note scale
vee-resonance.syllables: Vee Vee Vee Vee Vee Vee Vee Vee Vee
vee-resonance.technique: The labiodental V activates forward resonance and mask placement. Combined with the bright 'Ee' vowel, this exercise is a resonance powerhouse. Used by speech therapists and vocal coaches alike.
vee-resonance.demoDescription: Sing 'Vee' through the 5-note scale. The V consonant activates forward, mask resonance combined with bright placement.

tips.vowelShape: Bright, forward 'Ee' after the V onset. The V and Ee together create maximum forward resonance. You should feel strong buzzing in your front teeth and nose.
tips.breathSupport: The V creates slight resistance, so moderate pressure is needed. Think of the air 'leaning' forward into the V contact point. Steady, not forceful.
tips.mouthAndJaw: Upper teeth gently contact lower lip for the V, then release into the 'Ee.' The transition should be smooth — V naturally flows into Ee because both are forward sounds.
tips.posture: Standard singing posture. Focus on feeling the resonance in your 'mask' — the area around your nose, cheekbones, and forehead. That's where this exercise shines.
tips.commonMistakes: Not sustaining the V long enough to feel the buzz. Let the V ring for a moment before opening to Ee. Also, don't spread the Ee too wide — keep it focused and narrow.

wah-open-throat.vocalInstruction: Sing 'Wah' on each note of the 5-note scale
wah-open-throat.syllables: Wah Wah Wah Wah Wah Wah Wah Wah Wah
wah-open-throat.technique: The W glide naturally opens the throat by lowering the larynx and lifting the soft palate. Combined with the open 'Ah,' this exercise is excellent for releasing throat tension and creating spacious resonance.
wah-open-throat.demoDescription: Sing 'Wah' through the 5-note scale. The W glide opens the throat naturally for relaxed, spacious tone.

tips.vowelShape: Start with lips rounded for W, then open wide to 'Ah.' Feel the throat open and expand as you move from W to Ah — it's like opening a door from a small room into a cathedral.
tips.breathSupport: Relaxed, flowing air. This exercise is about opening and releasing, not power. Let the air flow freely through the open 'Ah' like a gentle river.
tips.mouthAndJaw: Lips round forward for the W, then jaw drops open for 'Ah.' Make the transition smooth and noticeable — you should feel the space in your mouth dramatically increase.
tips.posture: Relaxed, with attention to the throat area. Many singers unconsciously constrict their throat — this exercise counteracts that. Think of your throat as a wide, open tunnel.
tips.commonMistakes: Not opening enough on the 'Ah.' If your throat still feels tight, exaggerate the W-to-Ah opening. Also, don't skip over the W — let it do its work of opening the throat before you arrive at the vowel.

gentle-descending-hum.vocalInstruction: Hum gently from the 5th down to the root
gentle-descending-hum.technique: A gentle cool-down exercise. Softly hum from the 5th descending to the root. Keep it quiet, relaxed, and completely tension-free. This releases any remaining vocal tension after intensive practice.
gentle-descending-hum.demoDescription: Gently hum from 5th to root. A soothing cool-down that releases tension and returns the voice to a relaxed state.
vowelOptions: Mmm into Ah (open at the bottom)

tips.vowelShape: Lips gently closed, teeth slightly apart. The hum resonates in your nose and face. You should feel a pleasant buzz in your lips and cheekbones.
tips.breathSupport: Minimal. This is a cool-down — use as little effort as possible. Think of the sound fading gently, like a music box winding down.
tips.mouthAndJaw: Lips closed but not pressed together. Jaw slightly open behind the lips. Everything soft, gentle, and tension-free.
tips.posture: Relaxed, even seated is fine for cool-downs. Let your body settle. Some singers close their eyes during cool-down exercises to focus on the soothing sensations.
tips.commonMistakes: Making it too loud or effortful. Cool-downs should be the vocal equivalent of gentle stretching after exercise. If you hear yourself projecting, dial it back significantly.

low-range-sigh.vocalInstruction: Sigh gently from the octave down to the root
low-range-sigh.technique: Like a yawn-sigh but specifically for cooling down. Start at the octave and sigh down to the root on a breathy, gentle 'Ah.' Completely tension-free. Let gravity do the work.
low-range-sigh.demoDescription: Sigh from octave to root on a breathy 'Ah.' Gentle cool-down that releases all remaining vocal tension.

tips.vowelShape: Open, breathy 'Ah' — more air in the sound than tone. This is intentionally breathy. The air flowing freely helps relax the vocal folds after intense work.
tips.breathSupport: Almost none. Let the voice fall with gravity. The sigh should feel like releasing a held breath — effortless and relieving.
tips.mouthAndJaw: Completely relaxed, jaw hanging open. If you can yawn while doing this, even better. The yawn opens everything up for maximum relaxation.
tips.posture: As relaxed as possible. Seated, leaning back slightly — this is the vocal equivalent of lying on a massage table. Let everything go.
tips.commonMistakes: Adding too much voice. This should be more sigh than sing. If it sounds like a performance, you're trying too hard. Also, don't control the descent — just let the pitch fall naturally.

cool-down-lip-trill.technique: A gentle descending lip trill to wind down your voice. The bubbling releases remaining tension in the lips and jaw. Use minimal effort — this should feel like the vocal equivalent of foam rolling after exercise.
cool-down-lip-trill.demoDescription: Gentle descending lip trill. The bubbling releases tension and brings the voice to a restful state.

tips.vowelShape: Lips loose and floppy — like a horse blowing out. The looser your lips, the easier the trill. Don't try to shape anything behind the lips.
tips.breathSupport: Minimal, gentle airflow. Just enough to keep the lips buzzing. Think of the gentlest breeze — barely any effort.
tips.mouthAndJaw: Jaw hanging open behind the buzzing lips. Completely relaxed. If the trill stops, your lips are too tense — shake your face out and try again.
tips.posture: Fully relaxed. This is the last exercise of your session — let your body know it's done. Shoulders dropped, jaw loose, neck relaxed.
tips.commonMistakes: Trying too hard. If the trill won't happen, it's because you're tense — which means you need this exercise. Shake out your face, blow a big sigh, then try the trill again very gently.

quiet-sustained-oo.vocalInstruction: Hold a very quiet 'Oo' on a comfortable pitch
quiet-sustained-oo.technique: Pianissimo singing is the perfect cool-down. It requires minimal cord contact and gentle airflow. Hold a quiet 'Oo' on your most comfortable pitch. This is where you end your session.
quiet-sustained-oo.demoDescription: Sustain a very quiet 'Oo' at pianissimo. The ultimate cool-down — minimal cord contact, gentle airflow, total relaxation.

tips.vowelShape: Gently rounded lips for 'Oo.' Minimal opening. The sound should be small, intimate, and quiet — like humming to yourself in a peaceful moment.
tips.breathSupport: The gentlest breath you can produce. Barely any engagement. Think of fog gently drifting from your mouth on a cold morning — visible but effortless.
tips.mouthAndJaw: Small, relaxed opening. Lips gently rounded but not pursed tight. Everything minimal, everything soft. Less is more.
tips.posture: Completely relaxed. Seated is ideal for this final cool-down exercise. Close your eyes if you'd like. This is meditation through sound.
tips.commonMistakes: Singing too loudly. This should be the quietest you can sing while still producing tone. If you can hear yourself from across the room, you're too loud. Also, don't hold your breath — let the air flow gently.

chest-voice-power-scale.vocalInstruction: Sing 'Hey' with strong chest voice resonance
chest-voice-power-scale.syllables: Hey Hey Hey Hey Hey Hey Hey Hey Hey
chest-voice-power-scale.technique: Stay in your lower range and sing with full, grounded chest resonance. The 'Hey' onset is speech-like and powerful — think of calling out to someone. Build the foundation of your voice here.
chest-voice-power-scale.demoDescription: Sing 'Hey' with strong chest voice on the 5-note scale. Full, grounded sound — build your vocal foundation.

tips.vowelShape: Wide, open 'Eh' vowel with an H onset. Think of calling 'Hey!' across a room — bright, forward, and assertive. The sound should resonate in your chest.
tips.breathSupport: Strong, grounded support from the lower abs. Chest voice thrives on solid breath support. Feel your feet on the ground and sing from that foundation upward.
tips.mouthAndJaw: Jaw drops open assertively on 'Hey.' The H onset uses air, then the 'Eh' vowel opens up wide. Don't hold back — this exercise is about vocal confidence.
tips.posture: Grounded and powerful. Feet firmly planted, slight forward lean. Think of a speaker addressing a room — commanding but not aggressive.
tips.commonMistakes: Going too high and pushing into strain. Stay in your comfortable chest range — this isn't about high notes, it's about building resonance in your speaking range. Also, don't shout — volume comes from resonance, not force.

hey-call.vocalInstruction: Call out 'Hey!' on the root, then the fifth
hey-call.technique: Like calling across a room. Hit the root with a strong 'Hey,' then jump to the fifth. This builds projection, forward placement, and chest voice power through speech-like sounds. Natural and effortless.
hey-call.demoDescription: Call 'Hey!' on root and fifth — like calling across a room. Builds natural projection and chest voice power.

tips.vowelShape: Bright, assertive 'Eh' vowel. Think of genuinely trying to get someone's attention across a crowded room — that natural, unthinking placement is exactly right.
tips.breathSupport: Strong, instinctive support — the kind your body does automatically when you actually call out. Don't overthink it. Your body knows how to project; trust it.
tips.mouthAndJaw: Wide open, energetic. The H onset prevents glottal attack while the open vowel projects naturally. Each 'Hey' should feel like a natural speech gesture.
tips.posture: Energized, forward-leaning slightly. Think of your whole body participating in the call. This is a full-body exercise, not just a throat exercise.
tips.commonMistakes: Being too polite or gentle. This exercise only works when you commit to really calling out. Timid 'hey's won't build the projection you need. Also, don't strain — the power comes from the body, not the throat.

minor-third-jump.vocalInstruction: Sing 'La' on the minor third interval pattern
minor-third-jump.technique: Jump a minor third — the soulful, bluesy interval. Think of the first two notes of 'Greensleeves' or 'Hey Jude.' This interval training builds ear accuracy and vocal control for this essential interval.
minor-third-jump.demoDescription: Jump between root and minor third on 'La.' The soulful, bluesy interval — essential ear training.

tips.vowelShape: Open, relaxed 'Ah' with an L onset. The L gives a gentle, legato connection. Keep the vowel consistent between both notes — the interval changes, the vowel doesn't.
tips.breathSupport: Even and steady. The minor third is a small interval, so support should be consistent between both pitches. No extra push on either note.
tips.mouthAndJaw: Consistent opening. The jaw stays in the same position for both notes. All the pitch change happens internally — nothing moves externally.
tips.posture: Relaxed and focused. Interval training is ear work as much as voice work. Listen carefully to the distance between the notes. Can you hear the melancholy quality of the minor third?
tips.commonMistakes: Sliding between the notes instead of landing cleanly. Each pitch should be precise — like stepping on stepping stones, not sliding on ice. Use a piano or tuner to verify your intonation.

major-third-jump.vocalInstruction: Sing 'La' on the major third interval pattern
major-third-jump.technique: Jump a major third — the bright, happy interval. Think of the first two notes of 'When the Saints Go Marching In' or 'Kumbaya.' Clean, precise, and confident.
major-third-jump.demoDescription: Jump between root and major third on 'La.' The bright, happy interval — clean and precise landing.

tips.vowelShape: Same as minor third — open 'Ah' with L onset. The vowel is identical; only the interval changes. Notice how the major third feels brighter and more open than the minor third.
tips.breathSupport: Steady and even. Same principle as the minor third — consistent support, no surging. The slightly larger interval needs slightly more vocal fold adjustment, but not more air.
tips.mouthAndJaw: Consistent jaw position for both notes. Think of your mouth as a frame that stays stable while the picture inside changes.
tips.posture: Same as minor third — relaxed and attentive. Compare this exercise with the minor third version. Can you hear and feel the emotional difference between major (bright) and minor (dark)?
tips.commonMistakes: Singing the interval slightly flat. Major thirds have a natural tendency to go flat because singers are drawn toward the minor third. Use a tuner to confirm you're hitting the true major third.

minor-seventh-jump.vocalInstruction: Sing 'Nah' on the minor seventh interval
minor-seventh-jump.technique: A wide, dramatic interval. Think of the opening of 'Somewhere' from West Side Story. The minor seventh challenges both your range and your intonation. It's a big leap that must land precisely.
minor-seventh-jump.demoDescription: Jump between root and minor seventh on 'Nah.' A wide, dramatic interval that challenges range and intonation.

tips.vowelShape: Open 'Ah' with nasal N onset. The N keeps the sound forward, which helps accuracy on the wide leap. Don't change vowel shape between the low and high notes.
tips.breathSupport: Significantly more support needed for this wide interval. The jump from root to minor seventh spans almost an octave — prepare your support as you would for an octave exercise.
tips.mouthAndJaw: Allow the jaw to open more on the upper note. A minor seventh is a big interval — your body needs to accommodate the stretch. Think of a yawn opening into the higher pitch.
tips.posture: Grounded and stable. Wide intervals can pull singers off balance — stay centered. Don't reach up physically for the higher note; keep your body stable and let the voice do the traveling.
tips.commonMistakes: Landing flat on the seventh. This interval is tricky because it's close to the octave but distinctly different. Practice with a piano — play the two notes first, sing them back. Also, don't scoop up to the seventh; hit it cleanly from above.

pa-ta-ka.vocalInstruction: Alternate Pa-Ta-Ka on each repeated note
pa-ta-ka.syllables: Pa Ta Ka Pa Ta Ka Pa Ta Ka
pa-ta-ka.technique: Three consonants, three articulators: Pa (lips), Ta (tongue tip), Ka (tongue back). This is the gold standard of diction exercises, used in speech therapy and vocal training worldwide. Keep all three equally crisp.
pa-ta-ka.demoDescription: Alternate Pa-Ta-Ka on repeated notes. Activates lips, tongue tip, and tongue back — the complete articulation workout.

tips.vowelShape: Quick, open 'Ah' between each consonant. The vowel is just a vehicle — the consonants are the stars. Keep the 'Ah' short and clean.
tips.breathSupport: Moderate, with a puff of air on each plosive. Pa-Ta-Ka are all unvoiced, so they use more air than their voiced counterparts. Take good breaths between sets.
tips.mouthAndJaw: Three distinct articulator movements: Pa closes the lips, Ta taps the tongue tip behind the upper teeth, Ka lifts the tongue back to the soft palate. Each must be distinct and precise.
tips.posture: Alert and energized. This is a workout for your articulators — like going to the gym for your mouth. Stand with focus and intention.
tips.commonMistakes: Letting one consonant get lazy (usually Ka). All three must be equally crisp and distinct. Also, don't mumble — exaggerate each consonant, especially at speed. Clarity trumps speed.

tongue-twister-scale.syllables: Bri Bri Bri Bri Bri Bri Bri Bri Bri
tongue-twister-scale.technique: Consonant clusters force precise tongue coordination at speed. 'Bri' combines a lip closure (B) with a tongue tap (R) and a narrow vowel (Ee). This builds the diction clarity needed for singing fast lyrics.
tongue-twister-scale.demoDescription: Sing 'Bri' through the 5-note scale. Consonant clusters build precise tongue coordination for clear diction.

tips.vowelShape: Bright 'Ee' vowel following the consonant cluster. The Ee keeps the sound forward after the BR combination. Don't let it fall back into 'Bruh.'
tips.breathSupport: Moderate, with quick delivery. Consonant clusters use more air than single consonants, so take full breaths between runs. Keep the airstream consistent.
tips.mouthAndJaw: Complex articulation: lips close for B, tongue taps for R, tongue arches for Ee — all in rapid succession. Start very slowly and build speed as coordination improves.
tips.posture: Focused and alert. This is precision training for your articulators. Think of it like training fine motor skills — controlled, deliberate, building toward fluency.
tips.commonMistakes: Dropping the R when going fast (turning 'Bri' into 'Bee'). The R is the hardest part — it requires the tongue to tap quickly between the B and Ee. If you lose it, slow down.

rapid-consonant-switch.syllables: Bee Dee Gee Bee Dee Gee Bee Dee Gee
rapid-consonant-switch.technique: Each consonant shifts the point of articulation: Bee (lips), Dee (tongue tip), Gee (tongue back). Rapidly switching trains independent control of all three articulators — essential for singing fast, clear lyrics.
rapid-consonant-switch.demoDescription: Rapidly alternate Bee-Dee-Gee through the scale. Trains independent control of lips, tongue tip, and tongue back.

tips.vowelShape: Bright 'Ee' on all three syllables. The vowel stays the same — only the consonant onset changes. This isolates the articulator differences clearly.
tips.breathSupport: Light and quick. This exercise is about articulator agility, not breath power. Think of it as a tongue workout, not a lung workout.
tips.mouthAndJaw: Three different movements cycling rapidly: Bee (lips close), Dee (tongue tip taps), Gee (tongue back lifts). The challenge is switching cleanly between all three at speed.
tips.posture: Alert and engaged. Treat this like a coordination drill — focused, methodical, building speed gradually.
tips.commonMistakes: Blurring the consonants at speed. Each B, D, and G must be distinctly audible, even at tempo. If they blur, slow down. Also, don't add jaw movement to compensate for lazy tongue — the jaw should stay relatively still.

legato-phrase.vocalInstruction: Sing 'La' in one long, connected phrase
legato-phrase.technique: Sing a long, connected phrase over an octave and back. Focus on seamless legato — no breaks, no bumps, just a smooth line of sound from start to finish. This is the essence of beautiful singing.
legato-phrase.demoDescription: Sing 'La' in one seamless legato phrase over an octave. The essence of beautiful, connected singing.

tips.vowelShape: Open, consistent 'Ah' throughout. The vowel should sound the same at every pitch — this is harder than it sounds. As pitch changes, resist the urge to modify the vowel.
tips.breathSupport: Long, sustained, and carefully managed. This exercise tests your breath management over a full phrase. Take a deep, expansive breath and ration your air evenly across all the notes.
tips.mouthAndJaw: Stable and consistent. The jaw stays in the same relaxed, open position throughout. No changes, no adjustments — smooth and unchanging while the pitch moves freely.
tips.posture: Tall, open, and lifted. Think of your body as the instrument and the legato phrase as a bow being drawn across strings — one continuous, beautiful motion from start to finish.
tips.commonMistakes: Bumps between notes. Each note should flow into the next with no break, no accent, and no change in volume. If you hear separate notes, you're not achieving true legato. Think of pouring honey — slow, smooth, continuous.
